Name: Regulatory Environment-Illuminate Education-User
Description:
Regime protects students
Regime does not explicity protect teachers or administrators
Users rights of action are murky

Name: Threat Environment-Illuminate Education-User
Description:
Implementation users are familiar with threats to schools and students
Targets have high value, and are particularly vulnerable (students)

Name: Operational Environment-Illuminate Education-User
Description: High power distance: Hierarchy of users includes students, teachers, school staff/administrators, school district administrators

Name: Regulatory Environment-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Producer operates in a highly regulated environment, but regime applications are murky. Faces complex legal challenges because it is largely their clients that are responsible for regulatory compliance.

Name: Threat Environment-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description:
Producer should be familiar with education-related threats and vulnerabilities

Variable threat surface could depend on cloud vs. on-prem implementation structure

Name: Regulatory Environment-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Regime emerges from local, state, and national laws. State and national agencies have authority to act, and offer different protections.

Name: Operational Environment-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Broad and diverse user-base, resulting in disparate requirements for cybersecurity (e.g. by district, school, state gov, etc)

Name: Threat Environment-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: National agencies share familiarity, but state have different levels of expertise to handle cybersecurity

Name: Operational Environment-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Enforcement methods vary by agency/regime, ability to protect students from cybersecurity harms is complex (DepEd vs.FTC)

Name: Deploying-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Customer-users, including administrators, and IT staff, deployed the software to meet the needs of the primary end-users, teachers and students.

Name: Securing-Illuminate Education-User
Description: IT users responsible for deploying the software would have had to secure it based on industry best practices, as well as requirements set forth based on their regime. They would not have to follow security practices to update the software because it is cloud-based. End-users would observe basic cyber hygiene to protect themselves.

Name: Influencing-Illuminate Education-User
Description: User-advocacy groups attempt to influence regulators and software producers to improve protections, such as through litigation and corporate reform. They also try to influence users through education and training.

Name: Complying-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Guidance, rules, and best practices would mean that end-user permissions would be managed based on available software capabilities to meet regime requirements.

Name: Implementing-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Illuminate Education, a private/private equity company, started in 2009. To do business and grow, it acquired 12 other companies in 2016-2019, including IOeducation which built the PupilPath software.

Name: Securing-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Illuminate Education would have had to secure their disparate software solutions individually, as they were the result of serial acquisitions and did not appear to be integrated with each other.

Name: Influencing-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Illuminate Education participated in industry regulatory bodies.

Name: Complying-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description:
Illuminate Education would have had to implement technical solutions to comply with the regimes in the various jurisdictions in which they operated. For example, the "strict data agreement" with the NYC DOE. 

They also voluntarily signed onto the Student Privacy Pledge, an industry self-regulatory body.

Name: Rulemaking-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: There were multiple regimes that regulated the software producer's business operations, established through case and statutory law, primarily designed to protect data of students and minors under 18.

Name: Enforcement-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: The regulations provision the various responsible agencies with a variety of enforcement mechanisms should a conflict occur, and they were actively being used.

Name: Investigation-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Regulators also rely on investigation authority to determine enforcement approaches.

Name: Influencing-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Regulators, especially self-regulatory bodies like the Student Privacy Pledge, advocated for best practices by software producers. They also signaled their interests and shaped the regime through case and statutory law.

Name: Capacity-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Users had software that they used daily to meet their needs, supported at various levels by IT teams, budget, and other users. Software may have been seen to be on a spectrum of effectiveness depending on the user and specific implementation.

Name: Business Change-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Changing operations may have induced changes to the software deployment, and even could have impacted the software production

Name: Compliance-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Contracts, logs, training materials, audits, and other records were created based on national, state, local, and internal requirements to support the implementation.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Poor practices by users may have led to vulnerabilities

Name: Capacity-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Illuminate Education engineered and delivered software to its clients, although much of the knowledge and artifacts originated in companies it had acquired, and there had been layoffs prior to the incident.

Name: Business Change-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Producer acquired new companies, including staff and software

Name: Compliance-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Producer agreed to comply with regimes that govern their clients, and the individual contracts between them. Not all had requirements on encrypting data at rest, but it was still a best practice.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Complaints allege that Illuminate Ed did not follow data security best practices, encrypting data at rest

Name: Regimes-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Emergent regime is weak in enforcing student data privacy regulations, especially against third-parties

Name: Capacity-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Capacity was extremely limited at the outset of this case to handle data privacy issues for students and kids as a result of software-related incidents

Name: Posturing-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Affected users spoke out publicly about the breach. They connected with legal teams for lawsuits, and advocates for support, who also made statements.

Name: Compensating-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Parents of affected students sued in court for compensation, and had to find legal grounds to sue. Staff users referred to agreements for options.

Name: Correcting-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Contracts were canceled, and new software had to be found. End-users had to identify, deploy, and learn new software to correct for the loss.

Name: Complying-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Staff users followed the terms of hteir agreements, as well as the regime incl. national and local rules.

Name: Posturing-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Illuminate made limited public statements, and kept most information hidden, including court documents.

Name: Compensating-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: No compensation seems to have been offered or compelled to affected users. However, they were fined by regulators for $5.1m.

Name: Correcting-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Software was taken offline for about 10 days before it was restored. Internal changes must have been made to prevent such an attack from happening again.

Name: Complying-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: They would have adhered to agreement terms, such as NYCDOE's ability to terminate the contract. They may not have reported the incident in a timely manner. They settled with FTC and state regulators in 2025.

Name: Posturing-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Local regulators were outspoken about the event, national (such as FTC) made loose statements. Industry regulatory bodies also made statements. State AGs formed a new Consortium of Privacy Regulators and immediately soon took action against the company.

Name: Compensating-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: No compensation was mandated for users, however state regulators received fines paid by the company in 2025.

Name: Correcting/Enforcing-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Regulators canceled agreements, and one industry regulator removed them from their program. FTC filed a complaint and consent order 4 years later. State regulators levied fines in 2025 as well.

Name: Complying-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Regulators followed regime in place, canceling agreements, but taking little further action. Illuminate was not governed as strictly as the education bodies it served.

Name: Costs-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Users needed new software solutions when software went down, and if contracts were ended. Replacement software may have introduced new risks, especially free alternatives. Affected users are at risk of future harms like identity theft. Users also hired lawers out-of-pocket for lawsuits, but failed to get compensation.

Name: Transformations-Illuminate Education-User
Description: New relationships with edtech advocates were formed. Districts may have implemented new procedures.

Name: Remedies-Illuminate Education-User
Description: No compensation was collected. New prevention efforts were developed.

Name: Preservation-Illuminate Education-User
Description: Some school districts preserved their agreements with Illuminate.

Name: Costs-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Estimated cost of the remediation is $22m. $5.1m in penalties to state regulators were settled in 2025

Name: Transformations-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Likely put new procedures in place, but no public information is available. They were later acquired by a new private equity firm.

Name: Preservation-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Still in business, continued growth, and some customers continued with their service

Name: Remedies-Illuminate Education-Software Producer
Description: Illuminate Education did not receive any known remedies for suffering from the breach, it is possible they received an insurance payout.

Name: Costs-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Some resources were spent by state-level regulators to investigate, but no major costs were incurred for enforcement, remediation, etc.

Name: Remedies-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Some contracts were canceled. Courts refused to support the claims of affected users. An FTC Consent Order, and a multi-state effort, were used to force Illuminate to change practices.

Name: Transformations-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: Initially, only an industry regulator took any action, removing Illuminate from its program. In 2025 the FTC pursued enforcement actions, and a new coalition of state AGs formed and penalized the company for $5.1m.

Name: Preservation-Illuminate Education-Regulator
Description: The regime was largely preserved, but there were changes to laws and enforcement approaches.

Name: Regulatory Environment-ICRC-User
Description: Overarching regime protects beneficiaries to the utmost. However, national protections may be in conflict, and norms may not support use of software. Beneficiaries also have very little recourse to respond to security failures, or advocate for protections.

Name: Threat Environment-ICRC-User
Description: Threat surface varies widely by user-base, e.g. country and environment. Target value is variable, although users are highly vulnerable.

Name: Operational Environment-ICRC-User
Description:
Users rely on software for emergency situations, and use cases are heterogeneous, requiring customization. 
Hierarchy is medium power distance, users access software directly through Red Cross National Societies, but probably don't connect with developers at ICRC.

Name: Regulatory Environment-ICRC-Software Producer
Description:
Multiple regimes to adhere to, international and national for ICRC, plus national where software is deployed. Strict internal regulations stemming from history of the org. Complex technical challenges to deliver software. 

Failure to comply could mean they cannot deliver pre-emptively (blocked by national government), or could threaten users directly (e.g. if government wants access to data).

Name: Threat Environment-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: Highly variable threat surface, deployment is in different countries with different needs, different regimes, and different potential attackers.

Name: Operational Environment-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: Mission compliance over commercial viability. Must cooperate with on-site users (National Societies) for implementation.

Name: Regulatory Environment-ICRC-Regulator
Description: Internal and international regimes provide strict guards on actions. In-country requirements also force compliance activities pre-emptively, but are not likely to be safeguards for users. Emergent regime may conflict between those regimes.

Name: Threat Environment-ICRC-Regulator
Description: Governing bodies are unlikely to be more familiar with threats than producer. Governments may also be threat actors.

Name: Operational Environment-ICRC-Regulator
Description: Enforcement methods are limited, international regimes can only so much. Country regimes have political interests in ICRC operating in their country, driving much of their regulations.

Name: Deploying-ICRC-User
Description: Users include the National Society staff that deploy the software on site, and beneficiaries who use the features. NS staff are trained, and can request limited support and updates. Beneficiaries get value from the software, since it can be the only way to get in touch with separated family, but they may increase risk by using it.

Name: Securing-ICRC-User
Description: National Society staff that implement the software in the field take precautions to safeguard the data of the end-user beneficiaries. They also have heterogeneous familiarity with cyber hygiene.The beneficiaries may also try to protect themselves, but using the software opens them up to privacy harms.

Name: Influencing-ICRC-User
Description: All users have very few options to influence the data privacy and security laws of their host country or the internal regulations of the software producer, and do not take organized action to do so.

Name: Complying-ICRC-User
Description:
ICRC staff that deploy the software must comply with the overarching regime that governs the Movement, as well as that of the country where they operate, although those regimes may be complex and even in conflict. They also had to adhere to the supplemental guidance provided by the software producer.

End-users would need to follow rules brought to them by the deployment users.

Name: Implementing-ICRC-Software Producer
Description:
ICRC implemented case management software to help beneficiaries in disaster and conflict situations. They provide the software to affiliated National Societies that implement it.

The software was proposed in 2009 and launched in 2012, and is based on 150 years of work in their Restoring Family Links program, which helps reconnect families separated in conflict and emergency situations.

Name: Securing-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: The ICRC team followed best practices to protect their users, including both technical practices, and support for implementation users (National Societies).

Name: Influencing-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC and Movement are highly self-regulatory, promoting the interests of their beneficiaries internally. They partner with governments on humanitarian rights. However, they do not seem to influence cybersecurity laws in the jurisdictions where they operate.

Name: Complying-ICRC-Software Producer
Description:
ICRC must adhere to a uniquely complex regime designed for catasrophic situations which includes protecting user data, among many other concerns. They have a robust internal bureaucracy for creating and implementing their own policies, guidance, and rules. This were arduously documented.

They also deployed software to be compliant with the non-functional, regulatory requirements of their users.

Name: Rulemaking-ICRC-Regulator
Description: ICRC is heavily regulated by national and international regimes, but its self-regulation may be even more robust. Laws and regulations govern them, but they are driven by their own mission to protect their users. The regime evolves as disasters and conflicts continue.

Name: Enforcement-ICRC-Regulator
Description: Enforcement methods against ICRC and National Societies are limited. If they upset their host countries or other actors, they may not be allowed to operate. If they lose the trust of their beneficiaries, they may cease to be users.

Name: Investigation-ICRC-Regulator
Description: It does not appear that any regulatory body was engaged in investigation activities. Only the internal bureaucracy would have had oversight, but they did not have expertise/familiarity.

Name: Influencing-ICRC-Regulator
Description:
Changes to data security and privacy regimes were driven heavily by European regulators because of the scale of European users (NSs) of the ICRC software, and bolstered by the new GDPR rules. However, not all countries were subject to those regulators and rules. Different national regimes could also be impacted by regulator influence, which is hard to measure

Stakeholders in the ICRC Movement also had an impact on the rules governing the RFL software.

Name: Capacity-ICRC-User
Description: NS users implemented the software in unique contexts with various levels of resources, cultures of use, experience, and training, and with limited resources from the producer for support.

Name: Business Change-ICRC-User
Description: Each NS requires new customization and configuration, and they have changing needs

Name: Compliance-ICRC-User
Description: Training, documentation of processes, and terms and conditions for use were delivered and confirmed by implementation-users.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-ICRC-User
Description: Poor practices by users may have led to vulnerabilities, primarily to the beneficiaries they serve

Name: Capacity-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC had thorough documentation of the software and guidance to protect end-users. It was led by a team with expertise and engineered using leading software from Microsoft

Name: Business Change-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: No major business changes were precursors. The software launched in 2012 and grew in its use, but had limited change due to limited resources for development.

Name: Compliance-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC created its own rigorous process for adhering to principles that protect their users. They also tailored deployment for users based on national requirements.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: A new vulnerability was identified and a patch released, but ICRC failed to update for a month before it was exploited.

Name: Regimes-ICRC-Regulator
Description: The ICRC created a strict regime to govern their own data privacy. Countries where ICRC deployed may also have been under unique scrutiny.

Name: Capacity-ICRC-Regulator
Description: There is little capacity by external regulators to influence ICRC's software. However, third-party cybersecurity partners were active in supporting best practices, including sending alerts about a critical vulnerability that affected ICRC software. Capacity by nations varied.

Name: Posturing-ICRC-User
Description: The Movement made statements condemning the attackers, but did not outright name them.

Name: Compensating-ICRC-User
Description: None identified

Name: Correcting-ICRC-User
Description: Staff users made efforts to keep maintain capacity while the software was unavailable, such as reverting to previous processes like paper records or spreadsheets

Name: Complying-ICRC-User
Description: Users did not make any clear compliance efforts.

Name: Posturing-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC quickly and publicly addressed the breach, connecting with colleagues across cyber and aid industries.

Name: Compensating-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: None identified, but their interest is in protecting beneficiaries, so lack of compensation is still not ideal

Name: Correcting-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: Major efforts kept services running during the software outage. New safeguards were put in place by the Movement and called for its organizations to make changes.

Name: Complying-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC adhered to its regulations and best practices in this phase.

Name: Posturing-ICRC-Regulator
Description: No regulatory bodies made any public statements. But the ICRC did have an open, but private symposium where attendees could discuss their issues, but details were not released publicly.

Name: Compensating-ICRC-Regulator
Description: None identified

Name: Correcting/Enforcing-ICRC-Regulator
Description: The movement drafted and confirmed a new resolution for safeguarding their data.

Name: Complying-ICRC-Regulator
Description: The Movement as internal regulators, adhered to their principles in creating new safeguards to prevent a similar event from happening in the future. There does not seem to be any governmental regulatory action.

Name: Costs-ICRC-User
Description: Risk of future harm for compromised users. More difficulty in using services from ICRC.

Name: Transformations-ICRC-User
Description: None for end-users, but field workers may have adapted additional security practices.

Name: Remedies-ICRC-User
Description: No compensation. Field workers may have implemented new practices.

Name: Preservation-ICRC-User
Description: Users will still continue to use the RFL software for their needs in these unique situations.

Name: Costs-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: Remediation costs, creating a new cyber delegation. Also started hosting a symposium on data security.

Name: Transformations-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: New safeguards were put in place, and likely software updates. A new cyber delegation was created in Luxembourg.

Name: Preservation-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC continues to provide these services to meet its mission, and made public efforts to maintain trust.

Name: Remedies-ICRC-Software Producer
Description: ICRC did not receive any remedies as a result of the breach.

Name: Costs-ICRC-Regulator
Description: No new laws or regulations, but Movement did facilitate a process to develop and implement new safeguards.

Name: Remedies-ICRC-Regulator
Description: New safeguard processes put in place.

Name: Transformations-ICRC-Regulator
Description: A new cybersecurity delegation was created outside of ICRC's home, Switzerland. New guidance was developed.

Name: Preservation-ICRC-Regulator
Description: The existing internal regulations were maintained, and new ones were added

Name: Regulatory Environment-Equifax-User
Description: Regime protects consumers through a variety of frameworks, including financial, general consumer, and data privacy.

Name: Threat Environment-Equifax-User
Description: There is an extensive and pervasive threat environment. Massive amounts of valuable data are stored in a single place, with potential harm directly to users. The data is constantly threatened by nefarious actors using all possible means.

Name: Operational Environment-Equifax-User
Description:
Power distance is high, end-users are almost entirely passive. They do not have permission to opt-out of data collection by Equifax or its customers, except in extremely limited situations.

Customers are business-users, heterogenous across nearly all industries, and send consumer data  to Equifax so they can receive additional consumer data.

Name: Regulatory Environment-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Producer operates in a highly regulated environment, but regime applications are murky. Faces complex legal challenges because it is largely their clients that are responsible for regulatory compliance.

Name: Threat Environment-Equifax-Software Producer
Description:
Producer should be familiar with education-related threats and vulnerabilities

Variable threat surface could depend on cloud vs. on-prem implementation structure

Name: Operational Environment-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Broad and diverse user-base, resulting in disparate requirements for cybersecurity (e.g. by district, school, state gov, etc)

Name: Regulatory Environment-Equifax-Regulator
Description: The emergent regime is robust, and arises from multiple agencies with many mandates and enforcement methods available to them, from case and statutory law. They offer strong protections for consumers and regularly pursue regulatory action against Equifax AND its industry at large. However, they stop short of limiting business growth.

Name: Threat Environment-Equifax-Regulator
Description: The various agencies are familiar with the vulnerabilities and the potential harm to consumers, as well as best practices that Equifax should be following.

Name: Operational Environment-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Many enfocement methods are regularly employed, and can balance business interests with consumer protections. The political climate of the last 50+ years has frequently favored protecting consumer interests in the financial space. True for various regimes (US, CAN, UK).

Name: Deploying-Equifax-User
Description:
Business customers deploy Equifax software to inform their business decisions, while also returning consumer data to Equifax.

Consumer-users are the crux of the system, it is their data that is aggregated by Equifax for its customers to make business decisions. Consumers are laregly passive, cannot opt-out of the collection, and infrequently interact with the software, although it can have major impacts on their lives. Consumer-users' main interaction with the data is in major financial situations like buying a home, a car, or opening a bank account, when their credit score plays a role.

Name: Securing-Equifax-User
Description:
Business customers of Equifax may be able to submit their collected data securely, but the data they obtain about consumers must be secured in their own situation. Improperly secured reports present a risk to consumers, but the liability is on the customer.

Consumer-users must also be diligent to defend themselves against harms and prevent threats such as identity theft. Data security hygiene such as monitoring their credit scores, checking for suspicious behavior, keeping secure passwords, etc. is necessary to ensure that the data collected by Equifax has not ended up in the wrong hands.

Name: Influencing-Equifax-User
Description: User-advocacy groups attempt to influence regulators and software producers to improve protections, such as through litigation and corporate reform. They also try to influence users through education and training.

Name: Complying-Equifax-User
Description: Business customers that use Equifax's software are required to secure the data that they have access to because it is typically protected PII. There are compliance requirements for practices like disposal of data, training, and technical support.

Name: Implementing-Equifax-Software Producer
Description:
Equifax was in a sustained period of growth since the 1970s and the advent of modern computing. They introduced new lines of business by collecting, analyzing, and distributing vast amounts of consumer data to address the needs of their customers.

This also included growth in their software development practices.

Name: Securing-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: The data that they collected is extremely valuable to threat actors, whether for crime, hacktivism, espionage, or war. This meant leading cybersecurity practices had to be in place. However, the specific software that would get breached did not follow all best practices.

Name: Influencing-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: They did attempt to influence limited regulation to ease the costs of implementing security.

Name: Complying-Equifax-Software Producer
Description:
The financial industry and the credit reporting industry has some self-regulatory bodies to protect their businesses by protecting consumers.

The government also created strict regulations to protect consumer interests, including data collected by Equifax and its competitors.

As quickly as Equifax began to use computing for business in the 1970s, they also had to implement and maintain software solutions to comply with regulations, in particular, a dispute resolution platform to respond to consumer complaints.

Name: Rulemaking-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Strict rules regulating the credit reporting industry rose as quickly as the industry itself did, stemming from case and statutory law, with the input of politicians, consumers, advocacy groups, industry groups, lawyers, and judges. Rules continued to evolve, adapting to societal changes, with updates in likely every decade since 1970.

Name: Enforcement-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Enforcement practices were commensurate with rulemaking, agencies (especially the FTC) and courts took action against Equifax and other credit agencies for violations frequently.

Name: Investigation-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Investigation is used both before enforcement action and after and CRAs like Equifax are under constant scrutiny by elected officials ,agency bureaucrats, and advocacy organizations.

Name: Influencing-Equifax-Regulator
Description: The actions of the various regulators stand out as strong influence to encourage Equifax to adhere to rules, signaling their intentions, and shaping the regime. Successful enforcement actions promote the interests of the agency by showing their abilities, and bringing in revenue in the form of fines.

Name: Capacity-Equifax-User
Description:
End-users had nearly zero capacity, their data was collected and used.

Customer-users were heterogenous in their capacity to implement the software, across a spectrum of scenarios

Name: Business Change-Equifax-User
Description: Years of increasing data collection about end-users, customer-users implemention, and new lines of business

Name: Compliance-Equifax-User
Description: End-users are familiar with the value of their credit data, and regularly attempt to follow the rules when they may be harmed.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-Equifax-User
Description: Behaviors by users were not precursors to the incident

Name: Capacity-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Equifax is a large company, but had few resources dedicated to the software that was breached, including its security. It was partly an old system relying on dwindling staff that had experience with it.

Name: Business Change-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: The initial change was a feature created in the 70s, which was exposed to the internet much later, resulting in a complex application that was difficult to maintain

Name: Compliance-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Equifax complied with laws in creating the software initially, but did not continue later as the software evolved.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Equifax failed to patch their software for 2 months, leading to the breach. There were further security failures that exacerbated the impact of the incident.

Name: Regimes-Equifax-Regulator
Description: The ICRC created a strict regime to govern their own data privacy. Countries where ICRC deployed may also have been under unique scrutiny.

Name: Capacity-Equifax-Regulator
Description: There is little capacity by external regulators to influence ICRC's software. However, third-party cybersecurity partners were active in supporting best practices, including sending alerts about a critical vulnerability that affected ICRC software. Capacity by nations varied.

Name: Posturing-Equifax-User
Description: Users and advocacy groups made numerous public statements and began working together.

Name: Compensating-Equifax-User
Description: Users and advocacy groups filed lawsuits to seek compensation. Eventually they won.

Name: Correcting-Equifax-User
Description: Users could use privacy tools (including some from Equifax) to correct effects of the breach. But they are not able to opt-out of the software entirely. Business customers sought to change agreements to prevent future issues.

Name: Complying-Equifax-User
Description: Business customers investigated the failures and amended agreements.

Name: Posturing-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Created campaign to support users; made public statements; gave testimony to congress; made compensation promises

Name: Compensating-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Provided free services and eventually paid fines and compensation to affected users.

Name: Correcting-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Restored software, changed business processes (including firing staff), dedicated resources to making fixes. Worked with Mandiant to remediate.

Name: Complying-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Testified in Congress, followed consent orders; reported to Congress on results (via Mandiant).

Name: Posturing-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Strong public statements and non-binding actions were made quickly from across regulatory bodies. Agencies worked with each other and outside partners to interpret issues and address them.

Name: Compensating-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Multiple agencies pursued action and courts upheld their claims. Equifax was forced to pay monetary compensation in many venues.

Name: Correcting/Enforcing-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Many enforcement actions taken by many bodies. Equifax was investigated and punished in various ways.

Name: Complying-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Various agencies adhered to their mandates in taking actions: pursuing compensation, conducting investigations, making legal decisions.

Name: Costs-Equifax-User
Description: Users were at risk of future harms from the breach; they also had to take action to protect their privacy. Lawyers that (successfully) represented the users in courtwere paid for their efforts close to $80m.

Name: Transformations-Equifax-User
Description: Business customers may have changed their processes in dealing with Equifax and similar vendors.

Name: Remedies-Equifax-User
Description: Users received compensation from the court settlment, typically up to $125, with protection for up to $20k. They also received free privacy monitoring services.

Name: Preservation-Equifax-User
Description: Business customers seem to have continued business with Equifax. End-users still have no ability to opt-out.

Name: Costs-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Estimated costs range from $660m to $868m including fines, ad hoc activities, monitoring services. Compensation to US users totaled over $380m.

Name: Transformations-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Executive and senior leadership were fired, and new business processes were likely put in place.

Name: Preservation-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Equifax is still in business, and many business customers continued their relationship.

Name: Remedies-Equifax-Software Producer
Description: Equifax did not receive any known remedies for suffering the breach, they may have received an insurance payout.

Name: Costs-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Massive resources were put into regulatory actions from multiple agencies.

Name: Remedies-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Enforcement actions including investigation, fines, mandated compensation, ongoing monitoring, and engaging with networks to achieve these goals.

Name: Transformations-Equifax-Regulator
Description: No major transformations occurred, they mainly followed existing regime.

Name: Preservation-Equifax-Regulator
Description: Regulators preserved their mandate, showing that it is effective and can respond to issues governed by the regime.

Name: Regulatory Environment-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users had GDPR protections, but regime was limited regarding legal use of spyware. User advocacy groups were strong.

Name: Threat Environment-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users used a ubiquitous, free, consumer app. They were high value targets for a very specific attacker, and there was no real way to protect against this particular threat.

Name: Operational Environment-CatalanGate-User
Description: High power distance, and the software was in widespread use. Users could not add any security to this software, other than good hygiene practices.

Name: Regulatory Environment-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp is highly regulated through GDPR and had strong cybersecurity practices.

Name: Threat Environment-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp was familiar with a wide variety of threats and regularly released security updates. But given their massive user base, they were probably constantly under threat.

Name: Operational Environment-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Massive user base and widespread adoption on a variety of devices.

Name: Regulatory Environment-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: EU Regulators were mainly focused on data privacy for consumer technology. But there was limited attention to spyware threats. Spanish regulators did have processes in place to ensure spyware was used legally.

Name: Threat Environment-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: Regulators were familiar with cyber threats to privacy, mostly on data sharing and processing, and to some degree, hacking. EU regulators did not address spyware use actively, and Spanish regulators had regulations governing its use.

Name: Operational Environment-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: EU protections were available to pursue companies that violate GDPR, but had fewer options to pursue remedies for spyware harms. They also have an interest in protecting their ability to use spyware for state interests. Spain had resources to ensure regulations for the use of spyware were complied with.

Name: Deploying-CatalanGate-User
Description: Consumer users were active on WhatsApp and relied on it for personal and likely business use. Even among the targeted users, there would be vaious use cases, and not all of them were connected directly with each other.

Name: Securing-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users would have a mix of hygiene practices given the widespread use. However, even those practices could not protect them from zero-click vulnerabilities.

Name: Influencing-CatalanGate-User
Description: User advocacy groups were active in protecting user data security and privacy, including with spyware. In Europe these groups do have sway, but their spyware efforts were limited compared to consumer privacy.

Name: Complying-CatalanGate-User
Description: There was really no adherence capability for WhatsApp users. However, user advocacy groups were active in helping users follow best practices and documenting issues for at-risk groups like journalists and activists.

Name: Implementing-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp was a large and growing company, with an expanding user base. They rolled out new features regularly.

Name: Securing-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp and Meta had experienced security teams that regularly released patches and secured against threats.

Name: Influencing-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Meta has a large influence operation, but it was limited in its ability to impact regulations in Europe, although it tried aggressively.

Name: Complying-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Given its size, Meta was the focus of a lot of regulatory attention, which forced them to comply strictly with regulations.

Name: Rulemaking-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: The EU was very active in creating cybersecurity regulations, particularly for data security and privacy. The rules regarding the legal use of spyware were largely relegated to states, and Spain had a clear regime in place.

Name: Enforcement-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: The EU was very active in enforcing their GDPR policies, but was less active with regard to spyware.

Name: Investigation-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: EU investigative actions were quite thorough when it came to data privacy and security issues, especially with regard to Meta.

Name: Influencing-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: Regulators had strong influence in establishing their authority and protecting their interests, as well as shaping the regime.

Name: Capacity-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users, as a whole, would have little or no capacity for cybersecurity while using WhatsApp. However, they did have some capacity for activism.

Name: Business Change-CatalanGate-User
Description: The growing use of WhatsApp, in Spain and in Europe, and its regular release of new features made it useful for changing needs of its users.

Name: Compliance-CatalanGate-User
Description: There were no adherence or compliance outputs for WhatsApp users. But advocacy groups were documenting the use of spyware to inform future regulatory action.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-CatalanGate-User
Description: Poor practices by users were not the cause of the vulnerabilities

Name: Capacity-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp's capacity was very large and had dedicated resources to protecting its software.

Name: Business Change-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: The software was changing a lot, with new features released regularly, especially after its acquisition. It had a massively expanding user base.

Name: Compliance-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Meta and WhatsApp were required to comply with strict regulations, and did so.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Changes to software, a massive user base, provided a large threat surface and intricate unaddressed failures, in spite of their efforts.

Name: Regimes-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: The EU and Spain had strict regimes that were well defined for data privacy, but limited for spyware. Spain's regime was strict compared to some other countries using spyware.

Name: Capacity-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: The EU and Spain had capacity to address data privacy and security concerns for consumer companies like WhatsApp, and had demonstrated their abilities regularly.

Name: Posturing-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users and user advocacy groups were widely outspoken, and it was their collaborative work that broke the scandal.

Name: Compensating-CatalanGate-User
Description: Affected users have sought compensation through courts a few times, but no major cases have arisen.

Name: Correcting-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users have attempted to change the laws and fight against the threat of future harms from spyware attacks. They have also worked with other stakeholders, esp. WhatsApp, to improve security broadly.

Name: Complying-CatalanGate-User
Description: Affected users worked with civil society to use the regime to help their situation.

Name: Posturing-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Meta was outspoken about the incident and eventually railed publicly against the creators of the exploit. They called for better regulations to protect their software from attacks.

Name: Compensating-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Meta did not make any effort to compensate the affected users. But they did try to get compensation for their losses

Name: Correcting-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: It is likely that Meta enhanced their security practices based on the increasing threat of spyware. They also received support from human rights orgs in their case against NSO Group.

Name: Complying-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: Because they wanted to fight the future threat of spyware, they worked with regulators to make change.

Name: Posturing-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: EU regulators were outspoken and active about the incident, and worked closely with civil society.

Name: Compensating-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: No compensation to users has occurred, but WhatsApp did receive compensation for the attack.

Name: Correcting/Enforcing-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: A large enforcement and investigation campaign came after the breach, including the PEGA Committee and subsequent related legislation. But the legislation did not directly target the use of spyware. Spanish regulators also conducted investigations (enforcement) but made no changes to the law.

Name: Complying-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: EU regulators were diligent in adhering to their mandate, resulting in a thorough investigation. Spanish regulators also adhered to their mandate, investigating and eventually bringing to light important details about the case (although they ultimately found themselves in compliance).

Name: Costs-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users suffered data privacy breaches, may have had problems with court cases, were arrested, and probably had emotional struggles with their privacy being invaded.

Name: Transformations-CatalanGate-User
Description: New and deeper relationships formed with advocacy networks, resulting in new regulations

Name: Remedies-CatalanGate-User
Description: Users were not compensated for the breach. Some new preventative measures were put in place for journalists and for better security internationally. Nothing changed in Spain.

Name: Preservation-CatalanGate-User
Description: WhatsApp use continues to be widespread, but it is possible that some users changed platforms.

Name: Costs-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp was not forced to pay fines or damages. The security remediation seems to have been within the scope of their typical work. They may have suffered some reputational damage, but were also quite active in protecting their interests publicly and in court.

Name: Transformations-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: New precedent was set by their court case against NSO Group.

Name: Preservation-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: WhatsApp continued to grow and is still widely used. Security features continued to be enhanced.

Name: Remedies-CatalanGate-Software Producer
Description: New precedent was set by their court case against NSO Group.

Name: Costs-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: Regulators were aggressive in investigating and other enforcement activities, likely with large expenditures of resources.

Name: Remedies-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: New legislation was passed to help combat future spyware threats. They worked with civil society to help establish new regulations, and challenged other regulators that may not have been compliant.

Name: Transformations-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: International new cases, legislation, training, certifications, precedent, and statements resulted. Spain changed little.

Name: Preservation-CatalanGate-Regulator
Description: The existing system was largely maintained, but new best practices, and case law. Later, new regimes were created outside of EU and Spanish authority.

Name: Regulatory Environment-OPM-User
Description: Government employees are protected by strong regimes for their work. They are a massive workforce with many advocates. The sensitive PII being collected was protected under many regimes.

Name: Threat Environment-OPM-User
Description: As employees, data stored about them would have been a valuable target. As government employees, it was especially valuable, particularly for those with security clearances, and the background investigation data stored about them.

Name: Operational Environment-OPM-User
Description: There were many needs that the OPM served as the HR agency for the government, meaning that the client users of OPM had heterogeneous practices, and many complex software needs.

Name: Regulatory Environment-OPM-Software Producer
Description: The regime governing the OPM was strict, including CIO oversight and IG involvement. However, they were not strictly adherent, and reports indicate they were defying best practices and ignoring changes that needed to be made.

Name: Threat Environment-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM was in a risky environment given the sensitivity of the data they controlled. The growing threat of APTs also increased the riskiness. Their cybersecurity leadership seemed to be unfamiliar with the level of risk associated with their data.

Name: Operational Environment-OPM-Software Producer
Description: A major shift in their data and software to include the PSI systems added immense complexity to their systems. They were required to meet demanding user/client requirements to implement the system.

Name: Regulatory Environment-OPM-Regulator
Description: For years, government had established cybersecurity guidance and rules for government agencies. Requiring CIOs and IGs within agencies also supported a strict regime. However, this oversight did not translate directly into enforcement abilities.

Name: Threat Environment-OPM-Regulator
Description: Agencies responsible for government IT and cybersecurity were familiar with threats, particularly the growing threat from APTs.

Name: Operational Environment-OPM-Regulator
Description: Multiple agencies had clear methods of enforcement and resources to back their efforts. The climate was also conducive to ongoing regulatory activities.

Name: Deploying-OPM-User
Description: Clients of OPM were active in using the software provided to them to manage personnel, as well as the heavy demand for the PSI process and software.

Name: Securing-OPM-User
Description: Users of OPM software, including end-users and the "clients" would likely have been very active in securing their data given its level of importance. Additionally, they were also probably required to follow strict rules when collecting and processing the data.

Name: Influencing-OPM-User
Description: Users were somewhat passive in influencing security operations, although they did have representation e.g. from unions.

Name: Complying-OPM-User
Description: Using OPM as the HR service for government agencies, includin the PSI system meant agencies would have to comply with strict regulations to safeguard their data. The use of OPM in the PSI process was generally part of compliance efforts to improve operations and security.

Name: Implementing-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM was highly active, particulalry after taking over the PSI operations starting in 2004.

Name: Securing-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM had released a strategy cybersecurity plan but was identified as being one of the weakest implementers of cybersecurity practices among federal agencies. Their efforts fell below the minimum requirements, without which they may have done nothing to secure their systems.

Name: Influencing-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM was not involved in influencing cybersecurity regulations.

Name: Complying-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM was not in compliance with federal regulations or their internal processes. They almost entirely failed to secure their systems as required, and even mislead regulators about the status of their operations. They even attempted to hide behind regulations as a result of some failures, but did not consistently apply their efforts (FedRAMP).

Name: Rulemaking-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators were actively developing and implementing regulations to improve the security of federal agencies and mitigate ongoing risks.

Name: Enforcement-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators were active in enforcing regulations, including mandated reporting by agencies on their cybersecurity practices. However, they may have lacked some enforcement abilities which could have improved practices by agencies like OPM.

Name: Investigation-OPM-Regulator
Description: Investigations, such as audits, were near constant, including through the IG, as well as in regular testimony to Congress, and with the support of other agencies like the GAO.

Name: Influencing-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators were incredibly active in advancing cybersecurity through influence efforts, including cooperation between different branches and agencies.

Name: Capacity-OPM-User
Description: Clients made extensive use of OPM's PSI capabilities, and relied on them heavily to manage personnel, all of whom were government employees. Employees were represented by unions to help protect their privacy. But there was very little work that could be done to protect their privacy.

Name: Business Change-OPM-User
Description: Business change for OPM clients included the shift to using them for PSI processes. However, OPM had been responsible for personnel operations for a long time.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-OPM-User
Description: Poor practices by users may have led to some vulnerabilities, but it was more likely to be the software team

Name: Capacity-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM seemed to have nearly enough capacity to meet their overall needs, but were also limited by the amount of work they needed to complete and lackluster leadership. It could also rely on the support of other agencies, which it did not do adequately.

Name: Business Change-OPM-Software Producer
Description: There was high business change over the previous ten years, mainly the shift to managing the PSI process and technology.

Name: Compliance-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Adherence and compliance by OPM was substandard, and they regularly broke rules, in spite of warnings.

Name: Unaddressed Failures & Weaknesses-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Massive unaddressed failures and weaknesses, including ongoing failures over time led to the breaches.

Name: Regimes-OPM-Regulator
Description: The government maintained a strict regime to try and prevent data breaches, with enormous resources and thorough efforts towards prevention and accountability.

Name: Capacity-OPM-Regulator
Description: The government had enormous capacity to address potential cybersecurity failures and to ensure compliance by their agencies. However, some enforcement capacity, which was added later through CISA, was lacking at the time of the OPM breaches.

Name: Posturing-OPM-User
Description: Users were active in making statements about the breach, particularly with support of their advocate unions and other orgs

Name: Compensating-OPM-User
Description: Users sought compensation through lawsuits, represented by their unions. Cases were initially dismissed but proceeded after an appeal.

Name: Correcting-OPM-User
Description: Users had little recourse in correcting either the technology or the regime.

Name: Complying-OPM-User
Description: No documented compliance efforts

Name: Posturing-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM was active in attempting to distract from the breach to regulators, diminish the scope of the breach, and were forced to speak regularly about the issue in-depth.

Name: Compensating-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM initially provided credit monitoring for victims, but had limited options to go further than that without mandated actions. They eventually did have to pay modest damages to affected users.

Name: Correcting-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Correcting actions began in earnest to addresst the cybersecurity failures and technological issues after the 2nd breach was discovered. However, more could have been done, although they had some limitations.

Name: Complying-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Complying with oversight requirements, including reporting the breaches and following cybersecurity rules had some major issues.

Name: Posturing-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators from a variety of agencies were active in discussing the issues, particularly the House Oversight chair.

Name: Compensating-OPM-Regulator
Description: Courts mandated some compensation to users, but there were some limitations and even concerns about the credit monitoring expenses.

Name: Correcting/Enforcing-OPM-Regulator
Description: Investigations and enforcement efforts to bring OPM into compliance were rigorous.

Name: Complying-OPM-Regulator
Description: With so many regulations in place, many regulators were very active and followed their required practices in response to the breach.

Name: Costs-OPM-User
Description: The actual costs to users cannot be known, mainly for those who may have been harmed by espionage action. Some users did have direct harms and were compensated.

Name: Transformations-OPM-User
Description: New processes (or better adherence to existing ones) were established following the breach to better protect users.

Name: Remedies-OPM-User
Description: Users got some compensation, but not as much as other similar breaches. The highest risk users may have been even more negatively affected.

Name: Preservation-OPM-User
Description: User behaviors and requirements were not affected, no other options really existed. But future security measures were put in place.

Name: Costs-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Costs were high for OPM, including technical fixes, human resources, damage to reputation, and more. The costs of fixing were investigated.

Name: Transformations-OPM-Software Producer
Description: OPM drastically transformed their practices, and their leadership. Further changes to cybersecurity in the US gov were also a result.

Name: Preservation-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Eventually, some of the services performed by OPM were changed to another agency.

Name: Remedies-OPM-Software Producer
Description: Additional resources were allocated for OPM to improve cybersecurity.

Name: Costs-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators invested many resources to enforce and investigate the issues, as well as to help remediate future issues.

Name: Remedies-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators had a strong incentive to remedy the situation, mainly by improving security standards, but also in providing remedies to victims.

Name: Transformations-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulators enhanced their oversight and improved rules enforcing cybersecurity. Many rules were already in place, but this incident raised awareness considerably.

Name: Preservation-OPM-Regulator
Description: Regulation and enforcement were improved, and new precedence was set in dealing with these issues. But overall, the roles and rules stayed the same.
